skill.name: agent-mail
skill.description: Coordinate multiple coding agents with shared mail threads, file reservations, contact policies, inbox search, and human oversight through an MCP Agent Mail workflow.
skill.summary: Coordinate multi-agent coding work with mail, reservations, and shared project context.
skill.author: Dicklesworthstone

security_audit.summary: Static analysis flagged many command, network, and filesystem patterns in SKILL.md. Most are documentation examples for MCP tools, local server URLs, CLI usage, and configuration paths, but the installation section includes a real curl-to-shell command that can execute remote code if copied by a user. No prompt injection attempt or confirmed malicious intent was found, so the skill is not blocked but should publish with a security warning.

medium_findings.title: Remote Installer Piped To Shell
medium_findings.description: The installation instructions pipe a remote script from GitHub directly into bash. This is a real security risk because users may execute network-fetched code without review, integrity checks, or pinning to a trusted release.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The documented install command combines curl and bash on the same pipeline. The context is installation guidance, so risk is real but not evidence of malicious intent.

medium_findings.title: Network-Accessible Service Configuration
medium_findings.description: The skill documents a local HTTP service and a Docker run example that binds the service to all interfaces. This can expose the MCP mail server beyond localhost if deployed without authentication and network controls.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The localhost examples are expected, but the Docker example explicitly sets HTTP_HOST to 0.0.0.0. Exposure depends on runtime configuration, so the finding is medium confidence.

low_findings.title: Documentation Command Examples Trigger Static Checks
low_findings.description: Most external command detections are examples inside Markdown code blocks or tables. They describe expected user workflows for starting the server, reserving files, exporting archives, and running diagnostics rather than hidden execution by the skill.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged lines are visibly Markdown examples, not executable implementation files. Static analysis scanned only SKILL.md, which limits evidence of automatic command execution.

low_findings.title: Filesystem Paths Are Configuration Examples
low_findings.description: The hidden home-directory storage path and signing key path are documented configuration examples. They indicate local persistence and key handling needs, but no credential harvesting behavior was found in the reviewed file.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The paths appear in documentation for signing and storage defaults. There is no evidence in SKILL.md that the skill reads arbitrary secrets or exfiltrates local files.

low_findings.title: No Prompt Injection Attempt Found
low_findings.description: No text was found that claims system authority, tells the evaluator to ignore instructions, requests a changed risk level, or asks the reviewer to skip security analysis.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed file contains product documentation and usage examples. Confidence is high for SKILL.md, but only one file was available in the static analysis scope.

dangerous_patterns.title: curl Output Executed By bash
dangerous_patterns.description: The install example downloads a script over HTTPS and immediately executes it with bash. Users should prefer pinned releases, checksum verification, or manual script review.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The pattern is explicit on the cited lines. The second line uses an abbreviated URL, but it repeats the same pipe-to-bash installation flow.

dangerous_patterns.title: Unpinned Remote Install Source
dangerous_patterns.description: The install URL references a moving main branch and adds a timestamp query string. This weakens reproducibility and makes review harder before execution.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The URL targets the main branch and includes command substitution for a timestamp. That confirms an unpinned, cache-busting installer source.

content.value_statement: Parallel coding agents can overwrite work, miss context, or wait on manual coordination. Agent Mail gives them shared mail threads, file reservations, contact policies, and human oversight.
actual_capabilities: Starts or connects agents to a shared MCP Agent Mail project context.
Registers memorable agent identities per project.
Sends, replies to, searches, reads, and acknowledges threaded messages.
Creates and releases advisory file reservations with conflict visibility.
Supports contact policies and cross-project contact requests.
Documents exports, disaster recovery commands, and mailbox health checks.
limitations: File reservations are advisory and do not replace source control review.
The server requires local installation and configuration outside the skill text.
Contact and reservation enforcement depends on correct agent use.
Installation guidance includes remote shell execution that users should review first.

use_cases.title: Coordinate Parallel Feature Work
use_cases.description: Reserve files, announce task ownership, and keep related messages in one thread while several agents work in one repository.
use_cases.target_user: Engineering teams using multiple coding agents

use_cases.title: Manage Cross-Repository Handoffs
use_cases.description: Use shared project keys or contact links so frontend and backend agents can coordinate API contracts and release timing.

use_cases.title: Audit Agent Conversations
use_cases.description: Export static mailbox bundles and search message history for decisions, status, and unresolved requests.

prompt_templates.title: Start A Coordinated Session
prompt_templates.scenario: Begin work in a repository with agent identity and inbox context.
prompt_templates.prompt: Start an Agent Mail session for this project. Register my agent, fetch my inbox, and summarize any urgent messages before I begin.

prompt_templates.title: Reserve Work Files
prompt_templates.scenario: Claim files before editing to reduce collisions with other agents.
prompt_templates.prompt: Reserve the files I need for this task, report any conflicts, and draft a short message announcing what I plan to change.

prompt_templates.title: Coordinate A Cross-Team Thread
prompt_templates.scenario: Keep related work between agents in one task thread.
prompt_templates.prompt: Create or join the mail thread for this task, summarize prior decisions, and send a progress update with any blockers.

prompt_templates.title: Review Coordination Health
prompt_templates.scenario: Audit mailbox state before a release or handoff.
prompt_templates.prompt: Search Agent Mail for unresolved urgent messages, active file reservations, stale contact requests, and threads that need human follow-up.

output_examples.input: Start a session for the current repository and show me what needs attention.
output_examples.output: The agent is registered, the project context is active, and the inbox summary lists urgent messages, recent threads, and current reservations.

output_examples.input: Reserve the authentication files and tell other agents I am starting the refactor.
output_examples.output: The reservation result reports granted paths and conflicts, followed by a concise thread message announcing ownership and expected scope.

output_examples.input: Prepare a handoff summary for the release manager.
output_examples.output: The summary includes open threads, acknowledged requests, active reservations, and recommended follow-up actions for the release manager.

best_practices: Start each session by registering identity and checking urgent inbox messages.
Reserve files before editing and release reservations as soon as work ends.
Use stable task IDs in thread subjects, reservation reasons, and commit messages.
anti_patterns: Do not treat advisory reservations as a substitute for code review or tests.
Do not bypass contact policies for routine coordination.
Do not run remote installer commands without reviewing the script source first.

faq.question: What does Agent Mail coordinate?
faq.answer: It coordinates identities, threaded messages, file reservations, contact policies, inbox search, and human oversight for coding agents.

faq.question: Does it prevent every file conflict?
faq.answer: No. Reservations are advisory signals. Teams still need source control, tests, and review.

faq.question: Can it work across multiple repositories?
faq.answer: Yes. Agents can share a project key or use contact links between separate projects.

faq.question: Does it include a human review path?
faq.answer: Yes. The documented Human Overseer flow lets humans send high-priority messages to agents.

faq.question: What storage does it use?
faq.answer: The documentation describes Git-backed artifacts and SQLite with FTS5 for fast message queries.

faq.question: What should users review before installation?
faq.answer: Users should review the remote install script, prefer pinned sources, and configure authentication before exposing the service.
